title: Director's notebook — vision and design
summary: This lesson provides an in-depth guide to developing and articulating a directorial vision and corresponding design choices for the IB Theatre HL Director's Notebook. It focuses on the skills required for top-band achievement in Criteria B and C, emphasising coherence, justification, and intended audience impact.
learningObjectives: Define and formulate a coherent and insightful directorial vision for a chosen play text.
Translate an abstract directorial vision into concrete, integrated design concepts (set, costume, lighting, sound).
Justify design choices by consistently linking them to the directorial vision, specific moments in the text, and the intended impact on the audience.
Analyse and evaluate how different design elements work together cohesively to communicate meaning.
Understand and apply the language of the IB Theatre assessment criteria for the Director's Notebook, particularly for Criteria B (Vision) and C (Interpretation and Justification).

simpleExplanation.title: The Director as Theatrical Architect
simpleExplanation.summary: Your Director's Notebook is the blueprint for your production. Your 'vision' is the architectural style (e.g., minimalist, gothic, surrealist), and your 'design choices' are the specific materials, colours, and structures you use to build that world for an audience.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine you are an architect hired to design a building based on a client's story (the play text). Your 'directorial vision' is your core architectural philosophy for the project – perhaps 'a space that feels both public and intensely private'. Your 'design choices' are the specific blueprints: using one-way glass for walls (set design), installing harsh, fluorescent lights in some rooms and warm, hidden lamps in others (lighting design), and specifying the acoustics so that whispers travel (sound design). Every choice must be justified in your blueprint (the notebook) to explain how it serves the core philosophy and how it will make the inhabitants (the audience) feel and behave.
steps: Step 1: Interrogate the Text. Move beyond plot. Identify the play's central tensions, key themes, and 'problems' you want to solve. This forms the seed of your vision.

Step 2: Formulate the Vision. Write a single, powerful sentence that encapsulates your core interpretation. For example, 'This production will explore Medea not as a monster, but as a refugee pushed to an extreme psychological breaking point.'

Step 3: Brainstorm Design Concepts. For each design area (set, lighting, costume, sound), brainstorm ideas that physically manifest your vision. Think in terms of atmosphere, colour palettes, textures, and spatial relationships.

Step 4: Justify and Integrate. For every design choice, write a detailed justification. Use the formula: 'I will use [specific design choice] in order to [create a specific effect] which communicates [aspect of your vision] and makes the audience feel/understand [specific impact].' Ensure all your choices work together.

flashcards.back: The central artistic and intellectual framework for a production. It is a concise, arguable interpretation of the play text that guides all subsequent creative choices and aims to create a specific impact on the audience.
The tangible and practical application of the directorial vision. It's how the vision is translated into the physical world of the stage through staging, design, and performance style.

The written explanation of 'why' a specific choice has been made. A strong justification connects the choice to the directorial vision, a specific moment in the text, and the intended effect on the audience.

A high-level quality where all elements of the production (vision, design, staging, performance) are integrated and work together harmoniously to communicate a unified meaning. Examiners look for this 'golden thread'.
A crucial part of your justification. It's not enough to say what a choice 'shows'; you must articulate what you want the audience to think, feel, question, or understand as a result of that choice.

The arrangement of the stage and audience seating (e.g., proscenium arch, thrust, traverse, in-the-round). This is a fundamental design choice that heavily influences proxemics and audience relationship.
The use of space and distance between performers, and between performers and the audience, to communicate relationships, status, and emotional states.

A thin metal plate or piece of glass with a pattern cut into it, placed in a lighting instrument (a profile spot) to project a patterned beam of light, such as shadows of leaves, windowpanes, or abstract textures.
A large, curved curtain or wall at the back of the stage. It can be lit with different colours or projections to create a sense of infinite space, sky, or abstract emotional backdrops.
Sound that has a source within the world of the play and can be heard by the characters (e.g., a doorbell, a radio playing, dialogue).
Sound that exists outside the world of the play, for the audience's benefit only (e.g., an underscore of tense music, a symbolic sound effect). Characters cannot hear it.

flashcards.front: Top-band descriptor for Criterion B: 'Insightful and imaginative'
flashcards.back: This means the vision is not obvious or superficial. It demonstrates a deep, personal, and well-considered engagement with the play's complexities and possibilities, offering a fresh perspective.

flashcards.front: Top-band descriptor for Criterion C: 'Thoroughly and effectively justified'
flashcards.back: This means justifications are detailed, specific, and consistently link choices back to the vision and audience impact. The reasoning is clear, convincing, and demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of theatrical processes.

faq.q: What is the difference between a 'vision' and a 'concept'?
faq.a: Think of it as 'why' vs. 'how'. The vision is the core interpretive idea or argument about the play (the 'why'). For example, 'My vision is to show the characters in Waiting for Godot as digital avatars in a forgotten video game.' The concept is the practical and aesthetic plan for realising that vision (the 'how'). For example, 'The concept involves a set made of glitching LED screens, costumes with pixelated textures, and sound design using 8-bit chiptune music.'

faq.q: How artistic do my sketches need to be?
faq.a: Not artistic at all. The IB assesses your theatrical thinking, not your drawing ability. A simple, clearly labelled line drawing of a floor plan or a stick-figure costume sketch with detailed annotations is far more valuable than a beautiful but unexplained watercolour painting. Focus on clarity and communication of your ideas.

faq.q: Can my vision be totally different from how the play is 'normally' done?
faq.a: Absolutely. In fact, 'imaginative and insightful' visions (Criterion B) are often those that challenge traditional interpretations. The key is that your vision must be supported by the text. You can't ignore the play's events or themes, but you can interpret them through a new and exciting lens. Your justification is what proves your radical idea is theatrically viable and intellectually rigorous.

faq.q: How many design elements do I need to cover?
faq.a: You should aim to cover the main design areas: set, costume, lighting, and sound. However, the depth and focus may vary. Your vision might lend itself to a particularly detailed exploration of sound and lighting, with set and costume being more minimalist. The goal is to present a coherent and integrated world, showing how all elements work together, rather than treating each one as a separate, isolated task.

sections.content: The Director's Notebook is the most significant externally assessed component for HL students, worth 35% of your final grade. A substantial portion of these marks is awarded for your ability to articulate a compelling directorial vision and justify the design choices that bring it to life. This lesson moves beyond simply listing ideas and focuses on the critical skills of synthesis, justification, and demonstrating coherence—the hallmarks of a top-band notebook.
Criterion B: Forging an 'Insightful and Imaginative' Vision

Your directorial vision is the conceptual heart of your production. It is your central argument about the play. A weak vision is merely a summary of the plot or a generic theme (e.g., 'My vision is about love and hate'). A strong, 'insightful' vision offers a specific, arguable interpretation that will drive every choice you make. It should be born from a deep interrogation of the text's context, themes, character relationships, and structure.

items: Start with Questions: Don't start with answers. Ask 'Why this play, now?', 'What is the central conflict that interests me?', 'Whose story is this, really?', 'What feeling do I want the audience to leave with?'.

Develop a Vision Statement: Condense your ideas into a single, powerful sentence. For example, for 'The Crucible', a generic vision is 'It's about the Salem witch trials.' An insightful vision could be: 'My production will frame the hysteria not as a historical event, but as a viral social contagion, exploring how fear and misinformation can dismantle a community from the inside out.'

Vision vs. Concept: The vision is the 'what' and 'why' (the core idea). The concept is the 'how' (the practical application). Your concept might be 'The play will be set in a sterile, modern data-server room where whispers can be amplified and broadcast instantly.' This concept *serves* the vision of social contagion.

sections.content: Criterion C: 'Thoroughly and Effectively Justifying' Design Choices

This is where you connect your abstract vision to the concrete reality of the stage. For every design choice, you must explain how it functions to realise your vision and affect the audience. Generic justifications like 'the red light shows anger' will not score well. You must be specific and detailed, demonstrating a clear chain of thought from text to vision to design to impact.

sections.question: For a production of Sophocles' *Antigone*, you have formulated the following directorial vision: 'This production will present the conflict between Antigone and Creon not as right versus wrong, but as an inevitable collision between the unyielding, ancient laws of nature and the fragile, man-made structures of the state.' Justify your set design concept.
sections.solution: To realise my vision of a clash between natural law and state control, my set design will be built on a principle of hostile integration. The stage will be a thrust configuration, implicating the audience in the political debates. The floor and back wall will be constructed from polished black marble, etched with a faint grid pattern, representing the rigid, unforgiving order of Creon's Thebes. This surface will be cold, reflective, and hard, causing sound to echo sharply, reinforcing the harshness of Creon's decrees. However, piercing through this sterile environment will be the 'natural' element. A huge, ancient olive tree root system, made from gnarled, realistic silicone and wood, will aggressively break through the marble floor upstage centre, creating an uneven and treacherous playing space. This is where Antigone will perform her key monologues, physically occupying the space of 'natural law'. The roots will not be beautiful; they will be disruptive and dirty, suggesting that the laws of the gods are not gentle, but primal and uncontainable. This visual juxtaposition will ensure the audience does not see a simple hero/villain dynamic, but feels the tension of two powerful, incompatible forces occupying the same space. The uneven ground will force the actors to navigate the stage with care, physically manifesting the precarious balance of power in Thebes.

sections.content: Use a 'Justification Chain' for your key design moments. State the **Choice** (e.g., 'a single, bare bulb hanging over the table'). Link it to the **Vision** (e.g., '...to support my vision of poverty stripping away all artifice'). Explain the **Function** (e.g., 'The harsh, unfiltered light will expose every flaw and line on the characters' faces'). Finally, state the intended **Audience Impact** (e.g., '...making the audience feel like uncomfortable voyeurs, unable to escape the raw, bleak reality of the family's situation').

A top-tier notebook demonstrates how the design elements speak to each other. Your lighting should complement your set. Your costumes should exist logically within the world your set creates. Your sound design should enhance the atmosphere established by the lighting. This is coherence. When discussing your choices, create explicit links between them.

items: Create a Unified Palette: Think about the overall colour palette, texture, and 'world rules' for your production. Does your world use natural materials or synthetic ones? Is it saturated with colour or monochromatic? These rules should apply across all design areas.

Cross-Reference Your Ideas: When writing about your costume design, refer back to your set. For example: 'The muted grey, utilitarian costumes of the citizens will blend into the concrete walls of the set, visually suggesting their conformity and lack of individuality.'

Sound and Light Partnership: Discuss how sound and light will work in tandem. 'As the blue, sterile wash of light representing the state's control intensifies, a low, non-diegetic electronic hum will grow in volume, creating a sense of oppressive, inescapable surveillance for the audience.'

sections.content: While the Director's Notebook is primarily a written document, visual aids are essential for communicating design ideas. However, artistic skill is not assessed. The quality of your ideas and annotations is what matters. Clear, well-labelled sketches, floor plans, found images, and mood boards are excellent tools, but they are meaningless without detailed written justification that connects them to your vision and intended impact.

sections.question: For a production of Shakespeare's *A Midsummer Night's Dream*, your vision is to 'explore the forest as a subconscious, psycho-sexual dreamscape where hidden desires are unleashed'. Justify your costume design for Titania, Queen of the Fairies, with reference to her encounter with Bottom.
sections.solution: My costume for Titania will directly manifest her journey from regal authority to primal, subconscious desire. Initially, she will wear a structured gown made of iridescent fabric that catches the light, reminiscent of a dragonfly's wing, but with a stiff, high collar and corseted bodice, signifying her controlled power. The colours will be cool blues and silvers. However, as Puck's spell takes hold, her costume will be designed to deconstruct. The corset will have hidden ties that can be undone, and the outer structured layer will be torn away by the other fairies to reveal a softer, flesh-toned silk slip underneath. This 'under-layer' will be subtly embroidered with dark, vine-like patterns, as if the forest is physically growing onto her. For her encounter with Bottom, this transformation is complete. Her hair, initially in a severe updo, will be down and wild. The silk slip will appear slightly damp and smudged with dirt, linking her to the forest floor. This visual degradation of her regal status is not to make her seem pathetic, but to show her liberation into a more primal, instinctual being, fully embodying the 'psycho-sexual dreamscape' of my vision. The audience will witness a queen literally unravelling, forcing them to confront the play's themes of untamed desire rather than viewing it as a quaint fairytale.

sections.content: Avoid 'mood boards' that are just a collage of pretty pictures. Every single image included in your notebook must be explicitly annotated and justified. Why did you choose that specific texture? What does that particular shade of blue communicate? How does that architectural style inform your set? If you can't explain it, don't include it.

sections.label: Test Your Skills: Justify a Design Concept
